title: Fitting a mouse guard
subtitle: Protecting the winter colony from rodent damage.
excerpt: A mouse guard goes on in late September when the colony stops flying regularly. Field mice move into hives from October, nest in the cluster, and destroy combs over winter. This covers timing, how to fit the guard, and when to remove it in spring.
sourceNotes: NBU Beekeeper's Toolkit (2024), winter preparation. BBKA technical leaflet, winter management of honey bee colonies.

glossaryTerms.definition: The tight ball of bees that forms in the hive from October onward, generating heat by vibrating flight muscles. The cluster moves slowly through the food stores during cold spells.
The opening at the base of a hive through which bees fly in and out. The width is adjusted seasonally and for colony strength.

recipeTools.notes: For prying off any propolis seal before fitting.
Bees may still be flying in early autumn; wear a veil if uncertain.

content.text: Mice move into hives in autumn when outdoor temperatures drop and the colony is flying less. By October a field mouse can nest inside the hive body, destroy whole frames of drawn comb, and contaminate stored honey with droppings. A
guard with 8.5 mm holes lets foragers through but stops mice. It takes 10 minutes per hive and prevents damage that can set a colony back for the entire following season.
Late September is the target in most UK locations. The

has not yet formed but the colony is flying far less than in summer. Fitting the guard too early in warm weather can trap more bees at the entrance and cause heat build-up. Fitting too late risks mice moving in before the guard goes on. A good rule of thumb: after the ivy honey flow ends (usually mid to late October in the south, early October in Scotland and the north) is the outside limit.

Approach the hive from the side at a moment when flying is light (late morning on a cool day). Use the hive tool to scrape any propolis from the landing board that would prevent the guard from seating flat. Slide the metal or plastic mouse guard across the full width of the entrance, with the perforated strip sitting directly in front of the opening. On National hives, the guard clips or pins to the alighting board. On WBC hives, it tucks inside the porch entrance.
Check that there are no gaps at the corners. A mouse needs only a 20 mm opening to squeeze through; a guard that is not full-width is not effective.

Check the guard is still in place every time you pass the apiary. Woodpeckers and squirrels will pry at fittings; the occasional badger or muntjac can knock it off. If the hive is in an area with high woodpecker activity, wrap the outside of the hive body with wire netting (chicken wire stapled loosely around the brood box) before the guard goes on.

Remove the mouse guard in early spring, typically once the first warm days arrive and the colony is taking cleansing flights daily. February in mild southern regions; March in most of the UK. Leaving the guard on too long in spring restricts forager access when the colony is expanding rapidly, and can cause traffic congestion at the entrance.

attrs.title: Evidence of mouse presence
attrs.body: If you lift the roof in late autumn and find shredded grass or paper lining among the frames, check the brood frames carefully. Mice hollow out comb cells to use as a nest. Badly damaged frames should be removed and the wax rendered down; the bees will redraw them in spring if the frames are returned with fresh foundation.
